chunks.title: Resume Summary
chunks.text: Cloud architect and cybersecurity leader with 20+ years across DoD and enterprise environments. I design secure cloud platforms, build DevSecOps pipelines, and lead modernization efforts for mission-critical systems in regulated IL4/IL5/IL6 contexts.

chunks.title: Education
chunks.text: Bachelor of Science in Computer Engineering & Technology from Middle Tennessee State University (MTSU), Murfreesboro, TN, with a minor in Computer Science. Coursework covered embedded systems programming, digital logic design, and software development — the education that preceded cloud architecture and cybersecurity work across DoD mission environments.

chunks.title: Certifications & Credentials
chunks.text:
Microsoft Certified: Azure Solutions Architect Expert, Azure AI Engineer Associate. ISC²: Certified Cloud Security Professional (CCSP) – In Progress. Amazon: AWS Certified Solutions Architect – Associate. Security: CompTIA Security+. Networking: Cisco CCNP / CCNA Security / CCNA Data Center. ITIL: ITIL v3 Foundation.

Michael holds multiple cloud and security certifications including Azure Solutions Architect Expert, AWS Certified Solutions Architect Associate, CCSP (in progress), CompTIA Security+, Cisco CCNP/CCNA Security/Data Center networking certs, and ITIL v3 Foundation. These certificates and credentials validate his expertise across Azure, AWS, OCI/GCP multi-cloud platforms, zero-trust architecture, DevSecOps, and DoD IL5/IL6 security compliance.

Azure Solutions Architect Expert and AWS Certified Solutions Architect Associate certifications demonstrate hands-on mastery of Azure Government cloud architectures at IL5/IL6 levels alongside AWS GovCloud. Additional cloud credentials include Azure AI Engineer Associate covering AI/ML workloads in secure environments.

ISC² Certified Cloud Security Professional (CCSP) in progress, CompTIA Security+ validation of security fundamentals. These complement his practical experience securing DoD mission systems at IL4/IL5/IL6 levels with zero-trust architecture and defense-in-depth principles.

chunks.title: Infrastructure Architecture
chunks.text:
The chat assistant enforces per-IP rate limiting at two layers to protect the OpenAI backend from accidental abuse during heavy recruiter traffic. The first layer is in-memory, tracked per request timestamp with a 10-minute sliding window (configurable via CHATRATELIMITWINDOWSECONDS and CHATRATELIMITMAXREQUESTS environment variables — defaults are 600 seconds and 20 requests respectively). When that limit is hit the API returns HTTP 429.

The second layer is durable across Function instances, backed by Azure Table Storage using a fixed-window counter partitioned by sanitized IP hash. It handles cold starts and concurrent workers where in-memory state would otherwise diverge. Both layers fail open on storage errors so chat remains available during outages — availability takes precedence over strict quota enforcement for a public-facing recruiter tool.

Azure OpenAI itself applies its own RPM/TPM limits per deployment, managed via the Azure Portal or ARM templates. The site's Functions API consumes those quotas through managed identity, not shared secrets.

chunks.text:

chunks.title: Infrastructure Architecture
chunks.text: This resume site is built on a fully serverless, enterprise-grade Microsoft cloud stack. The architecture was intentionally designed to demonstrate hands-on experience with Azure services commonly used in large-scale government and commercial environments — the same patterns that power production systems at DoD IL4/IL5/IL6 compliance levels.

chunks.title: Infrastructure Architecture
chunks.section: Why Azure Static Web Apps (SWA) for Hosting
chunks.text: Azure SWA provides a globally-distributed, edge-cached static site without managing servers or load balancers. It handles HTTPS termination, CDN caching, and CI/CD from GitHub with zero infrastructure code. For a resume site that sees sporadic traffic spikes (recruiter review sessions), this means fast page loads everywhere without paying for idle capacity — the same cost model used for internal enterprise documentation portals at scale.

chunks.title: Infrastructure Architecture
chunks.text: The chat assistant, visitor counter, and content ingestion APIs run on Azure Functions — a serverless compute platform that scales from zero to thousands of concurrent requests automatically. Each function is deployed independently with its own resource allocation and security boundary: the chat endpoint uses managed identity for secure OpenAI access, while the visitor counter writes only to Table Storage with no inbound network exposure. This separation mirrors enterprise microservice patterns where each capability has a distinct threat model.

chunks.title: Infrastructure Architecture
chunks.section: Azure Table Storage for Persistent State
chunks.text: The visitor counter uses Azure Table Storage — a low-cost, NoSQL key-value store that scales linearly without database administration. For this use case (a single integer counter updated thousands of times daily), Table Storage is dramatically more cost-effective than Cosmos DB or SQL Database while still offering the same 99.99% availability SLA and encryption at rest. It's the kind of right-sizing decision that enterprise architects make when balancing features against total cost of ownership.

chunks.title: Infrastructure Architecture
chunks.section: Azure OpenAI with RAG for Intelligent Chat
chunks.text: The "Ask about my experience" assistant uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation — not a raw LLM call, but a pipeline where user questions are first semantically matched against indexed content (roles, education, certifications) using vector embeddings generated by Azure OpenAI's text-embedding model. Only the most relevant chunks are sent to GPT for answer generation, which means responses cite specific roles and avoid hallucination. The embedding vectors are pre-computed as static artifacts in this repository — a deliberate choice that eliminates runtime latency and cost while keeping the chat experience fast even under heavy recruiter use during hiring cycles.

chunks.title: Infrastructure Architecture
chunks.section: Content Pipeline: Markdown → RAG Chunks → Embeddings
chunks.text: Content lives as human-readable markdown files (site-v2/content/roles/*.md, summary.md, etc.). The build pipeline (scripts/build-rag.mjs) parses these into structured chunks with metadata, then a separate batch script generates vector embeddings against Azure OpenAI — handling rate limits (20 chunks per request), DNS workarounds for corporate network environments, and shell quoting safely. This separation of content authoring from ML processing mirrors how enterprise teams manage data pipelines: writers produce markdown, engineers maintain the indexing infrastructure.

chunks.title: Infrastructure Architecture
chunks.text: Every component uses Azure Managed Identities — no API keys stored in source control or environment variables at runtime. The Functions API is exposed only through SWA's built-in proxy (no public endpoint), and all storage accounts use private endpoints with network restrictions. This zero-trust architecture, where services authenticate to each other via managed identity rather than shared secrets, is the standard pattern recommended by Microsoft for production Azure deployments — including those subject to DoD IL5/IL6 compliance requirements.

chunks.title: Infrastructure Architecture
chunks.section: Why This Matters for Enterprise Recruiters
chunks.text: This stack demonstrates breadth across four categories of cloud engineering: infrastructure (SWA + Functions), data storage (Table Storage), AI/ML integration (OpenAI embeddings + RAG), and security/compliance (managed identity, private endpoints). Each choice was made with an understanding of trade-offs — cost vs. features, latency vs. complexity, security vs. convenience — which is exactly the decision-making that senior cloud architects perform daily in regulated enterprise environments.

chunks.title: Azure Architecture and Engineering Manager
chunks.company: Leidos - USAF Cloud One ACSS Program
chunks.text: Azure Architecture and Engineering Manager at Leidos - USAF Cloud One ACSS Program. Location: Remote (US). Dates: 2026-03 to Present. IL levels: IL4, IL5, IL6. Tags: Cloud Architecture, Engineering Leadership, DevSecOps, Zero Trust. Technologies: Azure, AWS, Terraform, Ansible, Jenkins, Kubernetes, Git. Highlights: Lead Azure architecture and engineering for the USAF Cloud One Architecture and Common Shared Services (ACSS) multi-cloud environment spanning Azure, AWS, Google, and Oracle. Manage and mentor a cloud engineering team delivering secure, cloud-native shared services across IL4/IL5/IL6 workloads. Drive DevSecOps, infrastructure as code, and zero-trust architecture to advance USAF system resiliency, security, and cost effectiveness.

chunks.text: Lead the Azure architecture and engineering practice for the U.S. Air Force Cloud One Architecture and Common Shared Services (ACSS) program, a global-scale, multi-cloud modernization effort supporting mission-critical Department of the Air Force workloads. Combines hands-on cloud architecture with engineering management, coordinating Azure delivery alongside AWS, Google, and Oracle across the program.

Design scalable, resilient, and secure Azure architectures and reference patterns for the Cloud One multi-cloud environment.
Manage, mentor, and supervise a team of cloud engineers, guiding cloud-native application design, deployment, and complex technical decisions.
Drive adoption of DevSecOps practices, infrastructure as code, and automation using Terraform, Ansible, Jenkins, and Kubernetes.
Architect secure cloud solutions implementing zero-trust principles and defense-in-depth across IL4/IL5/IL6 boundaries.
Manage Azure networking, VPNs, IaaS, PaaS, identity (Azure AD/SAML), and data services supporting shared-services delivery.
Partner with stakeholders to translate mission requirements into technical solutions and present architectural recommendations to program leadership.
